content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 26 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 112r–113v | **Source Images:** DEU_026_page_001.jpg; DEU_026_page_002.jpg; DEU_026_vaticanus_page_001.jpg; DEU_026_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** When you have entered the land the LORD your God is giving you as your inheritance and you have settled there, **2** take some of the firstfruits of all that you produce from the soil of the land the LORD your God is giving you. Place them in a basket and go to the place the LORD your God will choose as a dwelling for his name. **3** When you arrive, go to the priest who is serving at that time and say to him: "I declare today to the LORD your God that I have come into the land the LORD swore to our ancestors to give us."

**4** The priest shall take the basket from your hands and set it down before the altar of the LORD your God. **5** Then you shall declare in the presence of the LORD your God:

"My ancestor was a wandering Aramean. He went down to Egypt with only a few people and lived there as a foreigner, but there he became a great nation — powerful and numerous. **6** The Egyptians treated us harshly and oppressed us, forcing us into brutal labor. **7** We cried out to the LORD, the God of our ancestors, and the LORD heard our cry and saw our misery, our hardship, and our distress. **8** So the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm, with terrifying power, with miraculous signs and wonders. **9** He brought us to this place and gave us this land, a land flowing with milk and honey. **10** And now I am bringing the firstfruits of the soil that you, LORD, have given me."

Place the basket before the LORD your God and bow in worship before him. **11** Then you and the Levites and the foreigners among you shall rejoice in all the good things the LORD your God has given to you and your household.

**12** When you have finished setting aside a tenth of all your produce in the third year — the year of the tithe — you shall give it to the Levites, the foreigners, the fatherless, and the widows, so that they may eat to the full in your towns. **13** Then you shall declare to the LORD your God:

"I have removed the sacred portion from my house and have given it to the Levites, the foreigners, the fatherless, and the widows, exactly as you commanded. I have not deviated from your commands or forgotten any of them. **14** I have not eaten any of the sacred portion while I was grieving, nor have I removed any of it while I was unclean, nor have I offered any of it for the dead. I have obeyed the LORD my God; I have done everything you commanded me. **15** Look down from your holy dwelling place in heaven and bless your people Israel and the land you have given us as you promised our ancestors — a land flowing with milk and honey."

**16** The LORD your God commands you this day to follow these decrees and laws; be careful to obey them with all your heart and with all your soul. **17** You have declared today that the LORD is your God, that you will walk in his ways, keep his decrees, commands, and laws, and listen to his voice. **18** And the LORD has declared today that you are his treasured people, just as he promised, that you are to keep all his commands. **19** He has declared that he will set you in praise and fame and honor high above all the nations he has made, and that you will be a people holy to the LORD your God, as he promised.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 5 — "A wandering Aramean":** The Hebrew arami oved avi is the heart of this liturgy. PNEUMA renders oved as "wandering" rather than "perishing" — the more common translation — because the narrative context emphasizes Jacob's smallness and displacement, not his near-death. The confession moves from one man's vulnerability to a nation's emergence, making the gift of land all the more astonishing.

**Verses 5-10 — The historical creed:** This is Israel's earliest confessional formula, a compressed salvation history. PNEUMA keeps the first-person plural throughout ("we," "us") to signal that every generation participates in the memory. The worshiper does not merely recall the Exodus — they inhabit it.

**Verse 11 — Rejoicing together:** The Hebrew lists the worshiper, the Levite, and the ger (sojourner/foreigner) together at the feast. PNEUMA renders this as genuine communal celebration, not merely commanded ritual — the abundance of the land is meant to be shared across social boundaries.

**Verses 13-14 — The tithe confession:** This is an oath of clean conscience — the tithe was not contaminated by mourning rites, impurity, or use in funerary offerings to the dead. PNEUMA renders the three negatives as clear English statements of integrity.

**Verses 17-18 — Mutual declaration:** The Hebrew uses hig'darta ("you have caused to declare / made a declaration") symmetrically in both verses. PNEUMA renders this as "You have declared... the LORD has declared" to capture the covenant-exchange structure: Israel publicly claims the LORD as God; the LORD publicly claims Israel as his treasured people (am segullah). This is covenant-making as mutual pledge, not unilateral imposition.

— PNEUMA
